content.assessment: A piloted, comprehensive de-tracking approach shares the Polish reform's core structural mechanism (delaying selection through comprehensive lower-secondary schooling) but adds a staged implementation sequence — piloting before national rollout — that Poland's 1999 reform did not employ. This directly addresses the lens's implementation-sequence question: Poland moved to national implementation without a piloting phase, and its 2017 reversal imposed severe one-time system-wide costs; a pilot model could in principle allow evaluation and course-correction before comparable costs are incurred at scale. However, the lens's reversibility question remains a serious threat regardless of piloting: Poland's reform succeeded on outcome measures yet was reversed nationally, suggesting that even well-evidenced structural reorganization is not immune to political reversal, and a pilot's positive results may not secure durable political buy-in any more than Poland's national results did.
opportunities: A staged approach could allow observation of early implementation costs before committing to system-wide restructuring, unlike Poland's simultaneous national rollout
strengths: Staged pilot-before-national-rollout implementation sequence directly addresses the lens's implementation-sequence criterion in a way Poland's 1999 all-at-once reform did not, potentially avoiding equivalent-scale reversal costs if the pilot fails or if political consensus is lacking
Structural mechanism (comprehensive delayed selection) matches the lever shown to produce distributional gains in Poland's 1999 case
threats: Given Poland's reversal occurred despite measured success at a national level, a successful regional pilot provides no guarantee against equivalent political reversal risk once scaled nationally
weaknesses: Piloting does not resolve the core reversibility risk documented in Poland: national-level success did not prevent reversal, so there is no clear mechanism by which pilot success would secure durability at scale
